_description: Which RustPython field types OWN a garbage-collectable Python reference (and therefore must be visited by `Traverse::traverse`) versus which do not (safe to skip / no traverse needed). This is the false-positive control for the gc-traverse auditor: `#[pytraverse(skip)]` on a `counter: PyRwLock<BigInt>` is CORRECT (BigInt owns no Python ref), while a skip on `iterable: PyIter` would be a real leak. Derived from the Traverse impls in crates/vm/src/object/traverse.rs and audited against builtins/ payloads.
_matching: A field's type owns a ref if its type TEXT contains any `ref_owning_tokens` entry (word-ish substring), after the container wrappers in `transparent_containers` are seen through. `non_owning_leaf_types` are examples that must NOT match — none of them contain a ref-owning token, so the substring rule handles them; they are listed for the agent's benefit and as regression anchors. Cross-struct ownership (a field whose type is another local struct that itself owns refs, e.g. `inner: PySetInner`) is resolved intra-file by the scanner's local struct-closure, not by this table.

transparent_containers._note: Generic wrappers that own a ref IFF their type parameter does. The scanner sees through these — `PyRwLock<Vec<PyObjectRef>>` owns refs; `PyRwLock<BigInt>` does not. Traverse has explicit recurse impls for all of these (traverse.rs Vec/Option/Box/[T]/tuples; the locks use fallible try_read/try_lock + skip-on-fail).

non_owning_leaf_types._note: Leaf types that never own a Python ref — a `#[pytraverse(skip)]` on a field of one of these is correct and must NOT be flagged. None contains a ref-owning token, so the substring rule already excludes them; listed as documentation + regression anchors (the enumerate `counter: PyRwLock<BigInt>` skip is the canonical safe case).

traverse_contract._note: The `unsafe trait Traverse` contract (traverse.rs:26-42) the gc-traverse auditor enforces against manual `fn traverse` bodies.
rules: every owned PyObjectRef/PyRef must be passed to `traverse_fn` AT MOST ONCE — a miss leaks the object; a double-visit can panic/deadlock the collector
DO NOT clone a PyObjectRef/PyRef inside traverse() (bumps the refcount the collector is trying to measure)

a manual traverse that takes a lock must use the FALLIBLE try_read/try_lock and skip on failure (conservative keep-alive), never a blocking lock (deadlock risk) — see the PyRwLock/PyMutex impls at traverse.rs:111/133
